---
title: "GavinK_Comments"
output: html_document
---

Below contains some **specific things I noticed while performing the code-fold audit.** I have organized my thoughts by section (and only included sections where I noticed something).

See `reviewer-GavinK-CodeAudit.Rmd` for the code-fold audit itself.

One note: there were a number of places were I thought `ggplot2` could be formatted like it is elsewhere (i.e., \textcolor{red}{\textbf{ggplot2}}). I stopped listing these midway through chapter 4 as I was unsure if this was intentional and I was missing something. If this was not intentional, I am happy to edit instances of "ggplot2" for consistency.

# Chapter 3: Getting Started

## 3.1 --- Why plot your data?

### 3.1.2 --- One lousy point can ruin your day

> In this example, it was arguable that x and y axes should be reversed, to determine how well measured weight can be predicted from reported weight. In ggplot this can easily be done by reversing the x and y aesthetics.

- `ggplot` can be changed to ggplot2 and formatted the same as it is elsewhere (e.g., bold + red).

# Chapter 4: Plots of Multivariate Data

## 4.1 --- Bivariate summaries

### 4.1.1 --- Smoothers

> Non-parametric regression is a broad topic; see Fox (2016), Ch. 18 for a more general treatment including smoothing splines, and Wood (2006) for generalized additive models, fit using method = "gam" in ggplot2, which is the default when the largest group has more than 1,000 observations.

- 'ggplot2' here is bolded but not coloured red

### 4.1.2 --- Stratifiers

> In ggplot2 thinking, grouping is accomplished simply by adding an aesthetic, such as color = rank.

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

> The story in Figure 4.5 is again different. Faculty in applied disciplines on average earn about 10,000\$ more per year on average than their theoretical colleagues.

- 10,000\$ -> \$10,000

- "on average" appears twice

> For both groups, there is an approximately linear relation up to about 30–40 years, but the smoothed curves then diverge into the region where the data is thinner.

- Group `B: Applied` seems to plateau between 20-30 years

## 4.2 --- Data Ellipses

> 4.2 --- Data Ellipses

- "Ellipses" -> ellipses

### 4.2.1 --- Drawing data ellipses

***Label: fig-ellipses-coverage***

- The code for this figure is already folded; however, I am not sure if it should be. It is hard to glean what the text both directly before and after the figure is referring to without seeing the code.

### 4.2.4 --- Handling nonlinearity: Plotting on a log scale

> 4.2.4 --- Handling nonlinearity: Plotting on a log scale

- Capitalization after the colon is inconsistent with other labels (e.g., "4.5 --- Simpson's paradox: marginal and conditional relationships")

## 4.5 --- Simpson's paradox: marginal and conditional relationships

***Label: fig-peng-simpsons***

- This may be one of the figures Reviewer 1 was referring to as "too small"

> The ggplot2 code for the panels in this figure are shown below. 

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

## 4.6 --- Multivariate normality and outliers

### 4.6.2 Penguin data

> It is a bit tricky to label points selectively in ggplot2 when the criterion for which points to label is complex, or involves variables outside the data frame.

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

***Code that defines*** `peng.mod`:

- Chunk unlabeled, formatting of code does not appear ideally in the HTML version.

- This is *not* the chunk in the "Looking Ahead" callout in 4.7 

## 4.7 --- Scatterplot matrices

>  (The default colors are somewhat garish, so I use scales::hue_pal() to mimic the discrete color scale used in ggplot2).

- 'ggplot2' here is not bold/red

#### Looking Ahead

- `peng.lda` code has same formatting issues as the mentioned chunk containing `peng.mod` (see 4.6.2)

## 4.9 --- Generalized pairs plots

#### The species/sex/island penguin `pairs()` plot

***Label: fig-peng-mosaic***

- Species labels overlap. Perhaps fix by rotating labels?

## 4.14 --- Exercises

> 7. Other implementations of parallel coordinate plots in R include: MASS::parcoord(), GGally::ggparcoord() and PairViz::pcp(). The ggpcp version used here is the most general.

- ggpcp not coloured red like other packages

# Chapter 5: Dimension Reduction

## 5.2.2 --- Mathematics and geometry of PCA

> In this equation,

- Point #3 ("The column $\bf{v}_i$...") has formatting issues (see "_i\$")

## 5.2.5 --- Visualizing PCA scores and variable vectors

> The variable vectors (arrows) shown in Figure 5.11 have the following interpretations:

- See points 2 and 3 in the list under the above quoted text. Bullet points have formatting issues

## 5.3 --- Biplots

### 5.3.5 --- Supplementary variables

***Label: fig-crime-factominer***

- Font/figure is a little small

### 5.3.6 --- Example: Diabetes data

***Label: diabetes-scatter3d***

- Is there a way to format the labelling in this figure so that the text is more legible (e.g., reposition/recolour labels)?

## 5.4 --- Nonlinear dimension reduction

> The world of dimension reduction methods reflected by PCA is a simple and attractive one in which relationships among variable are at least approximately linear, ...

- "variable" -> "variables"

### 5.4.2 --- t-SNE

#### 5.4.2.1 --- Comparing solutions

> It is more interesting that the sizes and shapes of the group clusters change from one solution to the other. The normal group is most compact in the PCA solution, but becomes the least compact in t-SNE.

- Is a brief explanation as to why this occurs warranted? Or an explanation as to which solution is preferable?

# Chapter 6: Overview of Linear models

> Overview of Linear models

- "models" -> "Models"

## 6.1 --- The General Linear Model

> The General Linear Model

- "The General Linear Model" -> "The general linear model"

### 6.1.1 --- Model formulas

> (In this code, coef(workers.mod2) |> t() |> t() is used to print the coefficients as a one-column matrix.)

- Wondering if this block of text can be placed before the preceding chunk of code as opposed to after (with parentheses removed).

> So a worker with zero years of experience can expect an income of \$29162 and this should increase by \$1119

> This says that income is predicted to be \$23,068 with no experience, increase initially by \$2295, but that yearly increase decreases by \$330.

- Commas needed in dollar amounts

# Chapter 7: Plots for Univariate Response Models

## 7.4 --- Effect displays

### 7.4.1 --- Prestige data

***Label: fig-prestige-allEffects***

- *x*-axis ticks for `income` are overlapping substantially

## 7.5 --- Outliers, leverage, and influence

### 7.5.1 --- The leverage-influence quartet

***Label: levdemo1***

- `y = rep(...)` has no `times` argument

> Outlier: (52, 60) a low leverage point, but an outlier (O) with a large residual

- In ***Label: levdemo2***, `O` has `y = 65` instead of `y = 60` as described in-text

# Chapter 8: Topics in Linear Models

## 8.1 --- Ellipsoids in data space and $\bf{beta}$ space

### 8.1.2 --- Data ellipse and confidence ellipse

> Thus, controlling for Stress, the confidence interval for the slope for Coffee includes 0, ...

> In directions in data space where the slice of the data ellipse is wide—where we have more information about the relationship between Coffee and Stress, ...

- In above two passages, "Stress" and "Coffee" should be shown as `code`.

# Chapter 9: Collinearity and Ridge Regression

## 9.1 --- What is collinearity?

### 9.1.2 --- Data space and $\beta$ space

***Label: fig-collin-data-beta***

- *y*-axis labels are cut off on bottom 3 facets

## 9.4 --- Collinearity biplots

***Label: fig-cars-collin-biplot***

- Some of the text labels are obscured (e.g., by points or lines)

## 9.6 --- Ridge regression

> In many cases, analytical or computationally intensive methods have been developed to choose an optimal value of the tuning parameter, for example using generalized cross validation, bootstrap methods.

- "generalized cross validation, bootstrap methods." -> "generalized cross validation, [or] bootstrap methods."

# Appendix B --- R Code for Figures and Analyses

- Some of the labelling is inconsistent, e.g., '--' vs. ':' in the below:

> Davis data-- Models and plots
> Salaries data: scatterplots

- Davis-reg.R is also listed twice: once under Chapter 3 and once under Rcode

- Capitalization of first letter after emdash inconsistent, e.g.:

> levdemo.R --- Leverage and influence demo
> hatvalues-demo.R --- leverage and the data ellipse


<!-- I have read up to the start of  Chapter 10 -->
